The latter is formed by a large number of interacting genes, and can have multiple stable attractors corresponding to different cell phenotypes. Typically a large number of phenotype-specific genes maintain a specific phenotype through mutual activation while suppressing expression of genes corresponding to other exclusive phenotypes. In some sense it resembles a spin system segregating into upward and downward domains. When a cell phenotypic transition takes place, the genes need to switch their expression status, analogous to flipping some upward and downward spin domains. A question arises as to how this transition proceeds (Fig. 1B). The transition may be sequential with gene silence first to form an intermediate with the initial cell phenotype destabilized without commitment to a new phenotype, followed by activation of other genes to instruct the cell into one specific final stable phenotype (similar to the SN1 mechanism). Alternatively gene activation and silence may happen concurrently as in the SN2 mechanism, with hybrid intermediate states co-expressing genes corresponding to the two phenotypes. One can vision two qualitatively different characteristics of the two mechanisms. Compared to the initial and final states, the intermediate states tend to have increased number of smaller subsets together with lost gene-gene interactions for the sequential mechanism, but a fewer giant clusters reflecting an increased number of gene-gene interactions for the concerted mechanism. The latter also tends to have increased number of frustrated interactions at the intermediate states. Here we borrow the concept of frustration from spin glasses, and define an interaction Fij from an active gene j to a target gene i being frustrated if the expression status of gene i mismatches the nature of the interaction, e.g., activation acting on silenced gene i. While several existing studies have discussed frustration in undirected model network systems [3,4], here we set to examine the above two competing mechanisms against genome-wide network models reconstructed from single cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) datasets. We first analyzed a scRNA-seq dataset of epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) of human A549 treated with TGF-β[5], a total of N = 3003 single cell samples measured at several time points (Fig. S1a). During EMT cells change from epithelial to mesenchymal phenotypes with increased EMT hallmark gene set score. We selected M = 391 genes showing switch-like behaviors during EMT to form an M-dimensional state space (see SI for details). While scRNAseq data only provide snapshots of cell transcriptomic states, a recently developed RNA velocity analysis makes it possible to extract some dynamical information [6]. RNA velocity is a highdimensional vector that can be inferred from the quantity of spliced and un-spliced RNA, and predicts the future state of individual cells on a timescale of hours. Following Qiu et al. [7], from the RNA velocities we constructed a Markov transition model with an N × N transition matrix T specifying the transition probabilities among the N measured cells using a Fokker-Planck kernel (Fig.S1b). For rate theory analyses we estimated the kernel density of the day 0 samples using Scikit-learn [8], and defined the cells whose local densities 0d  are in top 100 as within the initial state A. Similarly we estimated the kernel density of the day 3 samples and selected those cells whose 3d  are in top 100 as within the final state B. We randomly selected pairs of cells in state A and the state B, and obtained an ensemble of Dijkstra shortest paths between them based on the transition matrix (Fig. 2a). We applied a modified finite temperature string method [9-11] to the ensembles of transition paths (see SI for details), and obtained an array of reaction coordinate (RC) points reflecting the average dynamics of simulated shortest paths (Fig.2b). The RC points divide the M-dimensional state space into Voronoi cells (Fig. S2a), so the value of RC of each cell was assigned by the Voronoi cell that it locates in. Next, to study how the regulatory network reconfigures along the RC, we need the governing dynamical equations of the EMT process. Qiu et al.[7] developed a procedure of reconstructing the generally nonlinear equations from the single cell expression vectors {x} and velocity vectors {v = (dx / dt)}, with α representing the α-th cell. Here we adopted a simpler linear model by assuming the governing equation as v =Fx+e , with ε being random white noises, and Fij quantifying the regulation of gene j on gene i so the node strength and direction in the intracellular gene regulatory network (GRN). The regulation can be direct such as gene j acting as a transcription factor on gene i, or indirect mediated through molecular species not resolved by the scRNA-seq measurements. We inferred the matrix F, which is in general asymmetric, from the data {x ,v}with the partial least square regression (PLSR) together with local false discovery rate (LFDR) methods to ensure F is sparse (see SI) [12]. Figure 2 Analyses on the EMT scRNA-seq data. (a) Dijkstra shortest path simulation on the transition graph represented in the 2D leading PCA space. Each path is a single cell trajectory (labeled with cyan triangle) that start from the epithelial state (labeled with magenta star) and transit into the mesenchymal state (labeled with lime cross). Each dot represents a cell. (b) RCs calculated from the Dijkstra shortest paths. The cyan dots are cells close to the RC to form a reaction tube, i.e., cells within each Voronoi cell that are k-nearest-neighbors of the corresponding RC point.. These cells are used to infer the F matrix. (c) Frustration score along the RC (s) of EMT. (d) Cell-specific variation of effective regulation edges in the GRN of EMT. Colors represent proportions of effective regulation edges to all edges in the GRN within each cell (represented as a dot). With F being the same for all cells, the effective GRN may differ between different cells. Notice that a prerequisite for gene j acting on i is that gene j is expressed in the cell, otherwise the j → i edge is treated as non-exist in this specific cell. Therefore with the gene expression binarized as 0 for silence, and 1 for active expression, we defined a frustration score for the interaction between a pair of genes (i, j) as fs ij = s j sgn (2s i -1)F ij ( ) , assuming a value 1 (not frustrated), 0 (no regulation), and -1 (frustrated), and sgn(x) is the usual sign function, sgn x ( ) = 1 if x > 0
papers.analogy_description: Introduces a method that treats steady states of gene regulatory networks as **thermodynamic macro-states** on a potential landscape, borrowing concepts from equilibrium and nonequilibrium thermodynamics to predict cell-state transitions in systems biology.
papers.problem: Understanding how cells transition between phenotypes by reorganizing their gene regulatory networks during processes like differentiation, specifically whether gene activation and silencing occur sequentially or concertedly.
papers.method_name: Chemical reaction mechanism analogy for cell transitions
papers.concrete_example: The paper explicitly models cell phenotypic transitions using the SN1 and SN2 chemical reaction mechanisms as analogs. In the SN1-like sequential mechanism, genes first silence to destabilize the initial phenotype (like breaking a bond first), then activate to form the new phenotype (like forming a new bond). In the SN2-like concerted mechanism, gene silencing and activation happen simultaneously (like the trigonal bipyramidal intermediate where bond breaking and formation occur together). The paper uses frustration scores to distinguish these mechanisms, finding that all three studied transitions follow the concerted SN2-like pattern.
papers.base_domain_justification: Organic chemistry, specifically the study of substitution reaction mechanisms (SN1 and SN2) that describe how chemical bonds break and form during molecular transformations. This field studies molecular-level phenomena governed by chemical kinetics and thermodynamics.
papers.target_domain_justification: Cell biology and systems biology, specifically the study of cell phenotypic transitions and gene regulatory network dynamics during processes like differentiation and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. This field studies cellular-level phenomena governed by gene expression and regulatory interactions.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by mapping the structural transformation problem from chemistry to biology: breaking/forming chemical bonds maps to silencing/activating genes, chemical intermediates map to transitional cell states, and the sequential vs. concerted mechanisms map to different orders of gene expression changes. The key transferred principle is that complex state transitions can proceed through fundamentally different mechanistic pathways (sequential vs. concerted), which can be distinguished by examining the properties of intermediate states.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Organic chemistry studies molecular transformations through chemical bond dynamics, while cell biology studies cellular state changes through gene regulatory networks. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study (molecules vs. cells), scales (nanometers vs. micrometers), and primary research communities, despite both involving state transitions.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper transfers the core mechanistic framework of SN1 vs. SN2 reaction pathways to structure its entire analysis of cell transitions. The chemical mechanism concepts directly inform the definition of frustration scores, the interpretation of intermediate states, and the central conclusion about concerted vs. sequential transitions. The analogy is not merely motivational but provides the fundamental conceptual architecture for understanding and analyzing the biological data.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Discovering this connection requires understanding that both domains involve multi-component systems transitioning between stable states through intermediate configurations, and that the order and timing of component changes (bonds vs. genes) fundamentally determines the mechanism. This is not obvious from keywords alone.
papers.well_known_justification: While both SN1/SN2 mechanisms and cell differentiation are well-studied topics, applying chemical reaction mechanism theory to analyze gene regulatory network transitions is a novel cross-domain application presented in this 2021 paper.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: This analogy requires a creative leap to map abstract mechanistic principles from organic chemistry (SN1 vs SN2 reaction mechanisms) to cell biology (gene regulatory network reorganization). The connection is non-obvious and counter-intuitive—recognizing that the fundamental distinction between sequential and concerted pathways in chemical bond rearrangements could illuminate the temporal ordering of gene expression changes during cell state transitions represents a sophisticated cross-domain insight that goes well beyond standard biological reasoning.

papers.title: Network Thermodynamics-Based Scalable Compartmental Model for Multi-Strain Epidemics
papers.abstract: SARS-CoV-2 continues to upend human life by posing novel threats related to disease spread and mutations. Current models for the disease burden of SARS-CoV-2 consider the aggregate nature of the virus without differentiating between the potency of its multiple strains. Hence, there is a need to create a fundamental modeling framework for multi-strain viruses that considers the competing viral pathogenic pathways. Alongside the consideration that other viral pathogens may coexist, there is also a need for a generalizable modeling framework to account for multiple epidemics (i.e., multi-demics) scenarios, such as influenza and COVID-19 occurring simultaneously. We present a fundamental network thermodynamics approach for assessing, determining, and predicting viral outbreak severity, which extends well-known standard epidemiological models. In particular, we use historical data from New York City’s 2011–2019 influenza seasons and SARS-CoV-2 spread to identify the model parameters. In our model-based analysis, we employ a standard susceptible–infected–recovered (SIR) model with pertinent generalizations to account for multi-strain and multi-demics scenarios. We show that the reaction affinities underpinning the formation processes of our model can be used to categorize the severity of infectious or deceased populations. The spontaneity of occurrence captured by the change in Gibbs free energy of reaction (ΔG) in the system suggests the stability of forward occurring population transfers. The magnitude of ΔG is used to examine past influenza outbreaks and infer epidemiological factors, such as mortality and case burden. This method can be extrapolated for wide-ranging utility in computational epidemiology. The risk of overlapping multi-demics seasons between influenza and SARS-CoV-2 will persist as a significant threat in forthcoming years. Further, the possibility of mutating strains requires novel ways of analyzing the network of competing infection pathways. The approach outlined in this study allows for the identification of new stable strains and the potential increase in disease burden from a complex systems perspective, thereby allowing for a potential response to the significant question: are the effects of a multi-demic greater than the sum of its individual viral epidemics?
papers.analogy_description: Develops epidemic dynamics by direct analogy to non-equilibrium thermodynamics, treating infection spread as free-energy consumption and entropy increase in an open system to derive a thermodynamic equation of motion for epidemics.
papers.problem: How to model and predict the severity of multi-strain viral epidemics (like SARS-CoV-2 variants) and multi-demics scenarios (multiple simultaneous epidemics like influenza and COVID-19), particularly to determine if combined effects exceed the sum of individual epidemics.
papers.method_name: Network thermodynamics for epidemic modeling
papers.concrete_example: The paper applies thermodynamic concepts to epidemiological transitions by calculating the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) for population transfers between susceptible, infected, and recovered compartments. The spontaneity of these transitions (negative ΔG indicating forward-occurring transfers) is used to categorize outbreak severity, with the magnitude of ΔG serving as a metric to examine past influenza outbreaks and infer mortality and case burden. Reaction affinities from chemical thermodynamics are mapped onto the formation processes governing disease transmission pathways.
papers.base_domain_justification: Chemical thermodynamics is the branch of physical chemistry that studies energy transformations in chemical reactions, including concepts like Gibbs free energy, reaction spontaneity, reaction affinities, and equilibrium states in chemical systems.
papers.target_domain_justification: Epidemiology is the study of disease distribution and determinants in populations, including the modeling of infectious disease spread through compartmental models like SIR (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered) frameworks.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by mapping population transitions in epidemic models (S→I→R) onto chemical reactions, where individuals moving between compartments are analogous to chemical species undergoing reactions. The thermodynamic principle of Gibbs free energy determining reaction spontaneity is transferred to assess the stability and likelihood of disease transmission pathways, with reaction affinities characterizing the driving forces behind population transfers just as they characterize chemical reaction propensities.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Chemical thermodynamics studies molecular energy transformations and equilibria in physical/chemical systems, while epidemiology studies disease spread in human populations. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study (molecules vs. people), distinct methodologies (laboratory experiments vs. population surveillance), and separate research traditions, despite both using mathematical modeling.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers core thermodynamic mechanisms and mathematical structures—specifically Gibbs free energy calculations, reaction affinities, and spontaneity criteria—to form the foundation of their epidemic severity assessment framework. These thermodynamic principles are not merely inspirational but constitute the central analytical tool for categorizing outbreak severity and predicting disease burden, making them essential to the paper's contribution.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Connecting thermodynamic spontaneity and Gibbs free energy to epidemic severity requires understanding the underlying mechanism of how energy minimization drives chemical reactions and mapping this structural principle onto population flow dynamics. This is not semantically obvious from keywords like 'reaction' or 'equilibrium' alone.
papers.well_known_justification: While network thermodynamics has been applied to biological systems, its specific application to multi-strain epidemic modeling appears to be a novel 2022 contribution rather than a classic textbook example.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While applying thermodynamic principles to epidemiology requires technical sophistication and creative insight, the analogy between population flows and chemical reactions is conceptually accessible once explained. The mapping is non-trivial but follows a logical structure where compartmental transitions naturally parallel reaction kinetics, making it more of a clever technical application than a deeply counter-intuitive leap.

papers.title: A new model for lipid monolayer and bilayers based on thermodynamics of irreversible processes
papers.abstract: Lipid monolayers are used as experimental model systems to study the physical chemical properties of biomembranes. With this purpose, surface pressure/area per molecule isotherms provide a way to obtain information on packing and compressibility properties of the lipids. These isotherms have been interpreted considering the monolayer as a two dimensional ideal or van der Waals gas without contact with the water phase. These modelistic approaches do not fit the experimental results. Based on Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes (TIP), the expansion/compression process is interpreted in terms of coupled phenomena between area changes and water fluxes between a bidimensional solution of hydrated head groups in the monolayer and the bulk solution. The formalism obtained can reproduce satisfactorily the surface pressure/area per lipid isotherms of monolayer in different states and also can explain the area expansion and compression produced in particles enclosed by bilayers during osmotic fluxes. This novel approach gives relevance to the lipid-water interaction in restricted media near the membrane and provides a formalism to understand the thermodynamic and kinetic response of biointerphases to biological effectors.
papers.analogy_description: Applies thermodynamic partition-function formalism to contact-process epidemic models, treating infection configurations as microstates to compute macroscopic outbreak properties analogous to thermodynamic ensembles.
papers.problem: Existing models for lipid monolayers and bilayers treat them as two-dimensional ideal or van der Waals gases without considering water phase interactions, failing to fit experimental surface pressure/area isotherms and explain osmotic behavior in biological membranes.
papers.method_name: Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes for lipid membranes
papers.concrete_example: The paper applies TIP formalism to model lipid monolayer expansion/compression as coupled phenomena between area changes and water fluxes, treating the monolayer as a bidimensional solution of hydrated head groups exchanging water with bulk solution. This coupling mechanism, originally developed for non-equilibrium transport processes in chemical systems, is mapped onto membrane behavior where mechanical compression (area change) is coupled to chemical potential gradients (water flux).
papers.base_domain_justification: Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes (TIP) is a branch of physical chemistry and non-equilibrium thermodynamics that studies coupled transport phenomena in systems far from equilibrium, such as heat-mass transfer coupling, electrokinetic effects, and chemical reaction-diffusion coupling in bulk solutions and chemical systems.
papers.target_domain_justification: Membrane biophysics is the study of biological membrane structure, dynamics, and physical properties, focusing on lipid organization, phase behavior, and interactions at biological interfaces. The field primarily investigates membrane phenomena using surface chemistry techniques and equilibrium thermodynamic models.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy transfers the fundamental TIP principle of coupled fluxes (where one driving force produces multiple effects) from chemical transport systems to membrane mechanics. The mechanical compression/expansion of lipid monolayers is mapped as being coupled to water transport, just as in TIP systems where temperature gradients couple to mass flow or chemical potential differences couple to electrical currents. The phenomenological coefficients and linear flux-force relationships from TIP provide the mathematical framework for describing membrane behavior.
papers.domain_distance_justification: While both domains involve thermodynamics, irreversible thermodynamics focuses on bulk transport phenomena in chemical systems, whereas membrane biophysics traditionally uses equilibrium surface chemistry and two-dimensional gas models. The domains share thermodynamic foundations but have distinct research questions and methodological traditions.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the core mathematical formalism of TIP, including coupled flux equations, phenomenological coefficients, and linear non-equilibrium relationships, to describe membrane behavior. This TIP framework forms the foundation of the entire model, replacing traditional equilibrium approaches with non-equilibrium coupled transport equations that quantitatively reproduce experimental isotherms.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that membrane compression behavior could be modeled as coupled irreversible processes requires understanding the mechanistic principle of flux coupling in non-equilibrium systems, not just semantic similarity between 'thermodynamics' terms used in both fields.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a novel 2019 application of TIP to membrane systems, not a classic textbook example of cross-domain transfer.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While applying thermodynamics of irreversible processes to biological membranes requires technical sophistication and recognizing that mechanical forces can couple to transport phenomena, this type of cross-domain application of TIP framework to biological systems was an established research direction by the mid-20th century. The analogy is not immediately obvious but follows logically from the general principles of coupled transport phenomena once the membrane is recognized as a non-equilibrium system.

papers.title: Early detection of metabolic and energy disorders by thermal time series stochastic complexity analysis
papers.abstract: Maintenance of thermal homeostasis in rats fed a high-fat diet (HFD) is associated with changes in their thermal balance. The thermodynamic relationship between heat dissipation and energy storage is altered by the ingestion of high-energy diet content. Observation of thermal registers of core temperature behavior, in humans and rodents, permits identification of some characteristics of time series, such as autoreference and stationarity that fit adequately to a stochastic analysis. To identify this change, we used, for the first time, a stochastic autoregressive model, the concepts of which match those associated with physiological systems involved and applied in male HFD rats compared with their appropriate standard food intake age-matched male controls (n=7 per group). By analyzing a recorded temperature time series, we were able to identify when thermal homeostasis would be affected by a new diet. The autoregressive time series model (AR model) was used to predict the occurrence of thermal homeostasis, and this model proved to be very effective in distinguishing such a physiological disorder. Thus, we infer from the results of our study that maximum entropy distribution as a means for stochastic characterization of temperature time series registers may be established as an important and early tool to aid in the diagnosis and prevention of metabolic diseases due to their ability to detect small variations in thermal profile.
papers.analogy_description: Applies the thermodynamic relationship between heat dissipation and energy storage—treating the body as a heat‐dissipating thermodynamic system—to derive thermal time-constant metrics as early diagnostic markers for metabolic and energy disorders.
papers.problem: Early detection of metabolic and energy disorders in rats fed high-fat diets by identifying when thermal homeostasis is affected, requiring a method to analyze core body temperature time series data for small variations that indicate physiological changes.
papers.method_name: Autoregressive stochastic model for thermal homeostasis
papers.concrete_example: The paper applies autoregressive (AR) time series models, originally developed for analyzing stochastic processes in signal processing and econometrics, to analyze core body temperature recordings in rats. The AR model uses maximum entropy distribution and stochastic complexity analysis to predict when thermal homeostasis would be disrupted by a high-fat diet, treating temperature fluctuations as a stochastic signal with autoreference and stationarity properties that can be modeled using the same mathematical framework used for financial time series or communication signals.
papers.base_domain_justification: Signal processing and time series analysis, particularly autoregressive models, originated in engineering fields (telecommunications, control systems) and econometrics for analyzing stochastic signals, financial data, and communication systems. These methods were developed to characterize, predict, and extract information from noisy sequential data.
papers.target_domain_justification: Metabolic physiology studies the biochemical and physiological processes related to energy metabolism, thermal regulation, and homeostasis in living organisms. This paper specifically focuses on detecting metabolic disorders through thermal regulation patterns in rats fed different diets.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by treating physiological temperature regulation as a stochastic signal process. The AR model's principle of characterizing sequential data through autoregressive relationships and maximum entropy distributions maps onto thermal homeostasis by modeling temperature fluctuations as having memory (autoreference) and statistical properties that can reveal underlying physiological state changes. The stochastic complexity measure from information theory transfers to quantify the disorder in thermal regulation.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Signal processing/time series analysis (engineering/mathematics) and metabolic physiology (biological/medical sciences) study fundamentally different phenomena—electronic/economic signals versus living organism metabolism—with distinct research traditions, though they can share mathematical tools.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the mathematical structure of autoregressive models, maximum entropy distribution, and stochastic complexity analysis from signal processing to physiological data. These are not merely conceptual inspirations but form the core analytical framework—the AR model equations, stationarity assumptions, and entropy calculations are applied directly to temperature time series as the primary methodological contribution.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that body temperature regulation can be modeled as an autoregressive stochastic process requires understanding the structural similarity between signal memory/prediction in engineering and physiological homeostatic mechanisms, not just keyword matching between 'time series' and 'temperature data.'
papers.well_known_justification: This is a novel 2014 application ('for the first time' as stated) of AR models to metabolic disorder detection through thermal analysis, not a classic textbook example.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While applying signal processing techniques to physiological data is an established practice in biomedical engineering, using stochastic complexity and AR models specifically for thermal regulation requires non-trivial insight into how information-theoretic measures can capture metabolic disorder. The connection is not immediately obvious but makes clear sense once the mapping between signal memory/entropy and homeostatic regulation is explained.

papers.title: PHYSIOLOGICAL FUNCTIONAL STATES OF MITOCHONDRIA IN THE THERMODYNAMIC AND ELECTROCHEMICAL CYCLE
papers.abstract: Aim.This study was performed to identify the possible physiological and pathogenetic processes taking place in the mitochondrial matrix which create the conditions for lithogenesis of insoluble calcium phosphate salts (calcium carbonate...). Whereas, they can later be deposited in various tissues, taking into account the fact that the formation of calcium phosphate (calcium carbonate ...) in the human body occurs under normal physiological conditions (bone tissue, otolith...). It raises the urgency of the question of understanding the physiological and pathogenetic mechanisms of lithogenesis.Materials and methods. There was carried out a meta-analysis of the functional states of mitochondria, to which we 125 Kubanskij nauchnyj medicinskij vestnik 2018; 25 (5) applied a mathematical model based on the changing direction and velocity of the conjugated thermodynamic and electrochemical parameters (pressure, volume, temperature, Gibbs potential, exergy…). Considering the schemes of the oxidative phosphorylation proposed by R.Mitchell and R.Williams, we created a model of the thermodynamic and electrochemical cycle of mitochondria which gives a deeper understanding of the principles of the mechanisms of the ongoing processes in the system mitochondrial matrix-internal membrane-intermembrane space.Results.Based on the fundamental principle of functional interaction, there were proposed four functional states of mitochondria (M) in thermodynamic and electrochemical (TD-EC) cycle, to which was created a mathematical model that allows to systematize the processes accompanied by the accumulation of the electrochemical potential, in other words, the charge separation (ionization) in the paramembrane space. At the same time, on the one side of the inner membrane (mitochondrial intermembrane space) the positive charge predominates, and on the other side (the mitochondrial matrix) – the negative. These processes, in view of the repulsion of like charges, lead to the increase in pressure both in the mitochondrial matrix and in the intermembrane space. In this sense, the direction of the electrochemical processes, taking place in the intramembrane and intermembrane environment from the position of physical thermodynamics, is similar to the direction of the processes occurring in the compressible ionized gas (plasma).The states of mitochondria are considered when the velocity of electrons along the respiratory chain, which is associated with a change in the thermal potential, changes in the thickness of its internal membrane. For the medium inside the matrix, which is an ultra-microheterogeneous dispersive mass, and also using the thermodynamic analogy with the ionized gas, by the thermal potential (Ǫ) we mean the product of pressure (P) per volume (V): Ǫ= PV. Based on the mathematical model of the thermodynamic behavior of the mitochondria and on the limitations imposed by the laws of physical and chemical thermodynamics, it is established that the greatest degree of thermodynamic perfection in the process of mitochondrial respiration corresponds to the state of "respiratory control" which, among the set of Functional States, is acceptable to consider the fundamental (basic, the first), in other words, F-I.The hierarchy of the homeostatic system of mitochondria is built according to the degree and speed of energy consumption which constantly switches (fluctuates ...) because life is the consequence of a stable nonequilibrium state of the special molecules, since living systems are never in equilibrium and, due to their free Gibbs energy (G), perform a constant work against the equilibrium.There is a physiological "balance" between the various functional states competing for the mitochondrial energy resources: 1) involuntary (Gibbs potential G>0) endergonic phosphorylation process which triggers ATP synthase and is accompanied by the cooling; and 2) spontaneous (Gibbs potential G<0) exergonic process that increases the temperature of the external medium. The pathophysiological "unbalance" of these mechanisms, in which the conditions for the formation of the watersoluble salt of calcium phosphate dihydrate-Ca (H2 PO4 )2 interchange with the poorly soluble calcium hydrogenphosphateСаHPO4 , can be a pathogenetic cause of the occurrence of common diseases (nephrolithiasis, osteochondrosis, atherosclerosis ...).Conclusion.In the thermodynamic and electrochemical cycle of the mitochondrial system matrix-internal membraneintermembrane space, the direction and speed of physiological functional variables, which determine the presence and magnitude of the primary physiological needs, are important. In the multidimensional space of the physiological functional variables there is a gap of functionality. This is the range of parameters variations, the limits of which are distributed according to Gauss and are optimal for the habitat mode in the external environment, which is the cytoplasm in regards to the mitochondria. Going beyond the limits of the gap of functionality promotes the thermodynamic and electrochemical adaptation changes in the mitochondrial system itself which tends to return to the state of the thermodynamic "rest", while the mitochondria performs a cyclic process.Relying on the fact that the fundamental principle of the functional expediency establishes the primacy of the maximum residence time of any living system in the defined ("normative", "permissible" ...) limits of the functionality gap, the fluctuations of which are conditioned by the changing external conditions and internal needs, we express confidence that, taking into account the limitations imposed by the laws of physical and chemical thermodynamics, the greatest degree of thermodynamic perfection of the mitochondrial breathing process in the dynamical electrochemical cycle is performed in the state of the respiratory control (F-I), which corresponds to the maximum entropy (S) and the minimum Gibbs energy (G). In the thermodynamic and electrochemical cycle may arise the conditions that include the adaptive biochemical changes that favor the accumulation of Ca2+ in the mitochondrial matrix, and thereby confirm the direct dependence of the calcium retention capacity on the speed of the respiration of mitochondria. At the same time, a significant amount of Ca2+ accumulates in the mitochondrial matrix, which, combined with the hydrophosphate, is transformed into the calcium diphosphate − Ca3 (PO4 )2 , which has an extremely low solubility in water. This may be a primordial mechanism of lithogenesis with the subsequent deposition of calcium phosphate salts in various tissues, causing the diseases at the organ level, in the pathogenesis of which the violation of energy metabolism is common!
papers.analogy_description: Builds a nonequilibrium thermodynamic model with coupled fluxes to represent adipose tissue metabolism, using transport and dissipation analogies to explain pathological fat accumulation in metabolic disease.
papers.problem: Understanding the physiological and pathogenetic mechanisms of lithogenesis (formation of insoluble calcium phosphate salts) in mitochondria, which can lead to diseases like nephrolithiasis, osteochondrosis, and atherosclerosis.
papers.method_name: Ionized gas (plasma) thermodynamic model for mitochondria
papers.concrete_example: The paper models the electrochemical processes in the mitochondrial matrix and intermembrane space as analogous to processes in compressible ionized gas (plasma). Specifically, charge separation creates positive charges in the intermembrane space and negative charges in the matrix, and the repulsion of like charges leads to pressure increases in both regions—similar to how ionized particles behave in plasma. The thermal potential is defined as Ǫ=PV (pressure times volume), directly borrowing from gas thermodynamics.
papers.base_domain_justification: Plasma physics and ionized gas thermodynamics, which studies the behavior of charged particles in gaseous states, including pressure-volume relationships, charge separation effects, and thermodynamic cycles in ionized systems.
papers.target_domain_justification: Mitochondrial bioenergetics and cellular biochemistry, which studies energy production, oxidative phosphorylation, electrochemical gradients across mitochondrial membranes, and calcium metabolism in cellular organelles.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works because both systems involve charge separation creating electrochemical potentials and pressure gradients. The repulsion of like charges in the mitochondrial paramembrane space (protons on one side, negative charges on the other) creates pressure increases similar to how charged particles in ionized gas create pressure through electrostatic repulsion. The thermodynamic cycle framework (pressure, volume, temperature relationships) transfers directly from plasma physics to model mitochondrial functional states.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Plasma physics studies ionized gases in physical systems (stars, fusion reactors, discharge tubes) while mitochondrial bioenergetics studies biochemical energy production in cellular organelles. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study, methodologies, and research communities despite both involving charged particles.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the mathematical framework of thermodynamic cycles from plasma physics, including pressure-volume-temperature relationships, thermal potential definitions (Ǫ=PV), and the treatment of charge separation effects. This forms the core mathematical model for analyzing mitochondrial functional states and is central to the paper's novel contribution of identifying four functional states in a thermodynamic-electrochemical cycle.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Connecting plasma physics thermodynamics to mitochondrial function requires understanding that charge separation mechanisms and pressure-volume relationships can operate similarly across vastly different physical scales and contexts—not obvious from surface-level semantic similarity between 'ionized particles' in different domains.
papers.well_known_justification: This appears to be a novel application of plasma thermodynamics to mitochondrial bioenergetics, not a classic textbook example of cross-domain transfer.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: This analogy requires a creative leap to map plasma physics concepts (typically applied to ionized gases in extreme conditions) onto mitochondrial bioenergetics, which operates in aqueous biological systems at physiological temperatures. The connection between electrostatic repulsion in charged particle systems and proton gradient dynamics in mitochondria is non-obvious and requires deep insight into the structural similarities between these disparate physical systems.

papers.title: Hybrid Ising Machine and Reinforcement Learning Approach to Folding Lattice Proteins
papers.abstract: The hydrophobic polarity (HP) protein folding model is a simplified computation model utilized to study the folding of proteins into their three-dimensional structures. Understanding how proteins fold and misfold is fundamental to designing effective, safe drugs, allowing for targeted therapies and better patient outcomes. The amino acids of a protein can be categorized as either hydrophobic or polar, where the hydrophobic beads tend to cluster together in a properly folded protein to minimize exposure to the surrounding environment. Determining the lowest energy configurations of the HP model is a non-deterministic polynomial (NP) hard problem. One method is formulating the HP model as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem which can be mapped to an Ising machine. In the Ising model, the lowest energy state of the system represents the correct lattice configuration of the folded protein. The Ising machine technique utilized of simulated annealing allows for future application of the work on an integrated silicon photonic chip. Alternatively, reinforcement learning designed with an energy-based reward function can optimize the HP model by adapting to the environment without the consequence of remaining in local minima like that of the Ising machine. A hybrid pipeline combining the Ising machine and reinforcement learning sequentially was designed to determine the two-dimensional lattice configurations of various protein chain lengths and sequences, combining the parallel optimization of the Ising machine on an integrated photonic platform and the adaptability of the reinforcement learning algorithm. Results found that this hybrid model improved the accuracy and ability of determining the lowest energy state in contrast to the Ising machine alone as well as improved the efficiency by reducing the number of iterations required when compared solely to reinforcement learning. Future work focuses on implementing a parallel pipeline approach as well as adapting to a three-dimensional model.
papers.analogy_description: Transfers the thermodynamic Ising model of spin systems to protein secondary structure elements, providing a novel structural biology model where residues behave like spins and cooperative transitions predict folding and designability.
papers.problem: Determining the lowest energy configuration of protein folding in the hydrophobic-polar (HP) model, which is an NP-hard computational problem requiring efficient optimization methods to find the correct three-dimensional structure of proteins.
papers.method_name: Ising model for protein folding
papers.concrete_example: The paper formulates the HP protein folding model as a quadratic unconstrained binary optimization (QUBO) problem which is then mapped to an Ising machine. In this mapping, the lowest energy state of the Ising spin system corresponds to the correct lattice configuration of the folded protein, where hydrophobic beads cluster together to minimize energy, analogous to how magnetic spins align to minimize energy in physical magnetic systems.
papers.base_domain_justification: Statistical physics, specifically the study of magnetic spin systems and the Ising model, which describes how magnetic dipoles (spins) interact and align to minimize energy in physical materials. This domain studies phase transitions, energy minimization, and collective behavior in physical systems.
papers.target_domain_justification: Computational biology and biochemistry, specifically the protein folding problem, which seeks to understand how amino acid sequences fold into three-dimensional structures. This domain studies biological macromolecules, their structural configurations, and the biochemical principles governing protein behavior.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy maps the energy minimization principle from magnetic spin systems to protein folding optimization. Just as spins in an Ising model interact with neighbors and seek the lowest energy configuration through alignment, amino acids in a protein interact with neighbors and seek the lowest energy configuration through spatial arrangement. The mathematical framework of the Ising Hamiltonian (energy function with pairwise interactions) directly transfers to represent the hydrophobic interactions and spatial constraints in protein folding.
papers.original_paper_info: The application of Ising models to protein folding was developed in earlier work, likely in the 1990s-2000s when researchers began formulating the HP model as optimization problems mappable to physical systems. This paper applies and extends that existing method by combining it with reinforcement learning.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Statistical physics studies physical magnetic systems and phase transitions in materials, while protein folding studies biological macromolecules and biochemical processes. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study (magnetic materials vs. biological molecules) and distinct research traditions, though they share mathematical optimization frameworks.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The Ising model's mathematical structure (Hamiltonian formulation, energy minimization through spin interactions) is directly transferred and forms the core computational framework for solving the protein folding problem. The QUBO formulation and simulated annealing approach are direct applications of the Ising model's mechanisms, not just conceptual inspiration. This structural transfer is central to half of the paper's hybrid approach.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that protein folding energy minimization can be solved using magnetic spin system dynamics requires understanding the underlying mechanism of pairwise interactions and energy landscapes in both domains, not just surface-level keyword matching between 'energy' terms.
papers.well_known_justification: The application of Ising models and QUBO formulations to protein folding and other optimization problems is a well-established approach in computational biology and quantum computing literature.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While the Ising model has been applied to protein folding in computational biology literature for several decades, making it a known connection, it still requires non-trivial insight to map spin interactions to hydrophobic forces and spatial constraints. The analogy is not immediately obvious to those outside computational biophysics, but once explained, the parallel between energy minimization in both domains is clear and well-motivated.

papers.title: The energy landscape theory of protein folding: insights into folding mechanisms and scenarios.
papers.analogy_description: Uses thermodynamic energy landscapes, originally from glassy systems and phase transitions, as an analogy to represent protein conformational space and guides bioinformatics methods for predicting folding routes and native-state structures.
papers.problem: Understanding how proteins fold from linear amino acid sequences into specific three-dimensional structures, and explaining the mechanisms and pathways by which this folding occurs despite the astronomical number of possible conformations.
papers.method_name: Energy landscape theory of protein folding
papers.concrete_example: The paper applies the concept of rugged energy landscapes from spin glass physics to protein folding, where the protein's conformational space is represented as a funnel-shaped energy landscape. The native folded state sits at the bottom of the funnel (global energy minimum), and the folding process is understood as a biased diffusion down this landscape, with the funnel topology ensuring that multiple pathways can lead to the native state without getting trapped in local minima.
papers.base_domain_justification: Spin glass physics is a subfield of statistical mechanics and condensed matter physics that studies disordered magnetic systems with competing interactions, characterized by complex, rugged energy landscapes with many local minima. This field developed mathematical frameworks for understanding how systems navigate complex energy surfaces.
papers.target_domain_justification: Protein folding is a fundamental problem in molecular biology and biochemistry that studies how polypeptide chains spontaneously fold into functional three-dimensional protein structures. This field investigates the biophysical mechanisms, kinetics, and thermodynamics of the folding process.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by mapping the conformational space of a protein onto the configuration space of a spin glass system, where both involve navigating complex energy landscapes with multiple minima. The key transferred principle is that the topology of the energy landscape (funnel-shaped vs. rugged) determines whether a system can efficiently find its ground state, explaining how proteins avoid Levinthal's paradox by having evolved smooth, funnel-like landscapes rather than randomly rugged ones like frustrated spin glasses.
papers.original_paper_info: The energy landscape theory was originally developed by Bryngelson and Wolynes in the late 1980s and early 1990s, with seminal papers around 1987-1989. Onuchic, Wolynes and colleagues further developed this framework throughout the 1990s. This 2000 paper appears to be a review/synthesis of the theory.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Spin glass physics studies magnetic materials and their phase transitions in condensed matter systems, while protein folding studies biological macromolecules and their functional structures. These fields have fundamentally different objects of study (magnetic systems vs. biomolecules), distinct experimental methodologies, and separate research communities, despite both using statistical mechanics.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The mathematical framework of energy landscapes, including concepts like frustration, roughness, and funnel topology, is directly transferred from spin glass physics to protein folding. The statistical mechanical formalism for analyzing how systems navigate complex energy surfaces forms the theoretical foundation of the protein folding theory, not just a metaphorical inspiration.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that the mathematical structure of spin glass energy landscapes could explain protein folding kinetics requires understanding the mechanistic parallel between magnetic frustration in disordered systems and conformational search in proteins—not obvious from surface-level keyword matching.
papers.well_known_justification: The energy landscape theory of protein folding is a landmark application that revolutionized the field and is widely cited in biochemistry and biophysics textbooks and literature.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: This analogy represents a creative leap connecting statistical mechanics of disordered magnetic systems to biological macromolecules—two seemingly unrelated domains. The non-obvious mapping of protein conformational space to spin glass configuration space, and the insight that landscape topology (not just energy minima) determines folding efficiency, required deep theoretical insight that fundamentally reshaped how scientists understand protein folding and resolved Levinthal's paradox.

papers.title: Mesoscopic Model and Free Energy Landscape for Protein-DNA Binding Sites: Analysis of Cyanobacterial Promoters
papers.abstract: The identification of protein binding sites in promoter sequences is a key problem to understand and control regulation in biochemistry and biotechnological processes. We use a computational method to analyze promoters from a given genome. Our approach is based on a physical model at the mesoscopic level of protein-DNA interaction based on the influence of DNA local conformation on the dynamics of a general particle along the chain. Following the proposed model, the joined dynamics of the protein particle and the DNA portion of interest, only characterized by its base pair sequence, is simulated. The simulation output is analyzed by generating and analyzing the Free Energy Landscape of the system. In order to prove the capacity of prediction of our computational method we have analyzed nine promoters of Anabaena PCC 7120. We are able to identify the transcription starting site of each of the promoters as the most populated macrostate in the dynamics. The developed procedure allows also to characterize promoter macrostates in terms of thermo-statistical magnitudes (free energy and entropy), with valuable biological implications. Our results agree with independent previous experimental results. Thus, our methods appear as a powerful complementary tool for identifying protein binding sites in promoter sequences.
papers.analogy_description: Uses a physical thermodynamic model of binding where each candidate site has an associated free energy and occupancy is given by a Boltzmann distribution, directly importing the partition-function analogy from equilibrium thermodynamics into a new computational method for predicting transcription factor binding sites.
papers.problem: Identifying protein binding sites in promoter DNA sequences is challenging because it requires understanding complex protein-DNA interactions at the molecular level. Traditional computational methods struggle to predict transcription starting sites and characterize binding dynamics with sufficient accuracy.
papers.method_name: Mesoscopic particle dynamics model for DNA binding
papers.concrete_example: The paper models a protein as a general particle moving along a DNA chain, where the particle's dynamics are influenced by the local DNA conformation determined by base pair sequences. The simulation tracks the joined dynamics of this protein particle and DNA, generating a Free Energy Landscape where the most populated macrostate corresponds to the transcription starting site in Anabaena PCC 7120 promoters.
papers.base_domain_justification: Statistical mechanics and thermodynamics, which study the behavior of particles in physical systems through energy landscapes, free energy calculations, macrostates, and thermo-statistical magnitudes. This field traditionally deals with physical particles, phase transitions, and equilibrium dynamics in non-biological systems.
papers.target_domain_justification: Molecular biology and biochemistry, specifically the study of protein-DNA interactions, gene regulation, and promoter sequences. This field focuses on biological macromolecules, transcription mechanisms, and binding site identification in living organisms.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy transfers the statistical mechanics framework of particle dynamics on energy landscapes to model protein-DNA binding. Just as physical particles explore energy landscapes and settle into low-energy states (macrostates), proteins are modeled as particles exploring DNA sequences, with binding sites corresponding to the most populated macrostates in the Free Energy Landscape. The thermo-statistical characterization (free energy, entropy) from physics is directly mapped onto biological binding dynamics.
papers.domain_distance_justification: While statistical mechanics and molecular biology are distinct fields with different primary objects of study (physical particles vs. biomolecules), there is established interdisciplinary overlap in biophysics. However, the research communities, methodologies, and traditional questions remain largely separate, making this a moderate rather than close transfer.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The core mathematical and conceptual framework of statistical mechanics—including free energy landscapes, macrostates, entropy calculations, and particle dynamics—forms the foundation of the solution. The entire computational method is built on transferring these physical principles to characterize protein-DNA binding, not merely using them as inspiration. The thermo-statistical magnitudes are central to both the analysis and biological interpretation.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that protein-DNA binding dynamics can be modeled using the same mathematical framework as particle dynamics on energy landscapes requires understanding the underlying mechanistic parallels between physical and biological systems, not just keyword matching between 'energy' or 'dynamics' in both fields.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a specific 2014 research paper applying statistical mechanics to cyanobacterial promoters, not a classic textbook example of cross-domain transfer.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: Applying statistical mechanics and energy landscape concepts to molecular binding is a well-established approach in biophysics and computational biology. This framework has been standard in the field for decades, appearing in textbooks and foundational papers on protein-DNA interactions.

papers.title: Information Thermodynamics of the Cell Signal Transduction as a Szilard Engine
papers.abstract: A cell signaling system is in a non-equilibrium state, and it includes multistep biochemical signaling cascades (BSCs), which involve phosphorylation of signaling molecules, such as mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) pathways. In this study, the author considered signal transduction description using information thermodynamic theory. The ideal BSCs can be considered one type of the Szilard engine, and the presumed feedback controller, Maxwell’s demon, can extract the work during signal transduction. In this model, the mutual entropy and chemical potential of the signal molecules can be redefined by the extracted chemical work in a mechanicochemical model, Szilard engine, of BSC. In conclusion, signal transduction is computable using the information thermodynamic method.
papers.analogy_description: Models multistep biochemical signaling cascades as a Szilard engine, transferring information‑thermodynamics concepts of work extraction, mutual information, and generalized second law to derive a new quantitative framework for cell signal transduction.[2]
papers.problem: How to quantitatively describe and compute the thermodynamic properties of biochemical signal transduction cascades in cells, particularly the energy extraction and information processing during multi-step phosphorylation pathways like MAPK signaling.
papers.method_name: Szilard engine model for signal transduction
papers.concrete_example: The paper models biochemical signaling cascades (BSCs) as Szilard engines, where a presumed feedback controller (Maxwell's demon) extracts chemical work during signal transduction. Specifically, the mutual entropy and chemical potential of signal molecules in MAPK pathways are redefined using the extracted chemical work in this mechanicochemical model, treating each phosphorylation step as analogous to the information-to-work conversion in a Szilard engine.
papers.base_domain_justification: Information thermodynamics is a field of physics that studies the relationship between information processing and thermodynamic work, particularly through thought experiments like the Szilard engine (a single-molecule heat engine) and Maxwell's demon, which explore how information can be converted to extractable work.
papers.target_domain_justification: Cell signaling is a field of molecular and cellular biology that studies how cells transmit and process biochemical signals through cascades of molecular interactions, particularly phosphorylation cascades like MAPK pathways that regulate cellular responses to external stimuli.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by mapping the information-to-work conversion mechanism of the Szilard engine onto biochemical signal transduction. Just as a Szilard engine uses information about a particle's position to extract work via a feedback controller (Maxwell's demon), the cell signaling cascade uses information encoded in molecular states (phosphorylation) to drive biochemical work through controlled energy extraction at each signaling step.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Information thermodynamics is a physics domain studying abstract thermodynamic engines and information theory, while cell signaling is a biological domain studying concrete molecular interactions in living cells. These fields have fundamentally different objects of study (thought experiments vs. biochemical pathways) and distinct research communities.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper transfers the core mathematical and mechanistic structure of the Szilard engine to cell signaling, redefining fundamental quantities like mutual entropy and chemical potential based on the Szilard engine framework. The information-to-work conversion mechanism and the role of Maxwell's demon as a feedback controller form the foundation of the computational approach for signal transduction thermodynamics.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that biochemical signaling cascades can be modeled as Szilard engines requires understanding the underlying mechanism of information-to-work conversion and feedback control, not just surface-level keyword matching between 'information' and 'signal' or 'energy' and 'thermodynamics'.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a specialized 2018 application of information thermodynamics to cell signaling, not a classic textbook example like Maxwell's demon itself or other well-established cross-domain applications.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: This analogy requires a creative leap connecting an abstract thought experiment from statistical mechanics (Szilard engine/Maxwell's demon) to biochemical processes, mapping information-theoretic concepts onto molecular signaling in a non-obvious way. The connection between feedback control in thermodynamic engines and phosphorylation cascades is counter-intuitive and represents a novel theoretical framework rather than a standard application.

papers.title: Thermodynamics of Computational Copying in Biochemical Systems
papers.abstract: Living cells use readout molecules to record the state of receptor proteins, similar to measurements or copies in typical computational devices. But is this analogy rigorous? Can cells be optimally efficient, and if not, why? We show that, as in computation, a canonical biochemical readout network generates correlations; extracting no work from these correlations sets a lower bound on dissipation. For general input, the biochemical network cannot reach this bound, even with arbitrarily slow reactions or weak thermodynamic driving. It faces an accuracy-dissipation trade-off that is qualitatively distinct from and worse than implied by the bound, and more complex steady-state copy processes cannot perform better. Nonetheless, the cost remains close to the thermodynamic bound unless accuracy is extremely high. Additionally, we show that biomolecular reactions could be used in thermodynamically optimal devices under exogenous manipulation of chemical fuels, suggesting an experimental system for testing computational thermodynamics.
papers.analogy_description: Treats biochemical receptor–readout signaling networks as thermodynamic copying devices, importing Landauer‑style bounds and work–dissipation relations from thermodynamic computation theory to characterize energetic limits and optimality of cellular information copying.[3]
papers.problem: Understanding the fundamental thermodynamic costs and efficiency limits of biochemical copying processes in living cells, specifically whether cellular readout mechanisms that record receptor states face the same thermodynamic constraints as computational copying operations.
papers.method_name: Computational thermodynamics framework for biochemical copying
papers.concrete_example: The paper analyzes a canonical biochemical readout network where readout molecules (R) record the state of receptor proteins (L) through binding and unbinding reactions. It applies Landauer's principle from computational thermodynamics to show that correlations generated between the receptor state and readout molecules set a lower bound on dissipation, just as copying information in a computer requires minimum energy dissipation. The paper demonstrates that biochemical networks face an accuracy-dissipation trade-off analogous to but worse than the computational bound.
papers.base_domain_justification: Computational thermodynamics is the field studying the fundamental thermodynamic costs of information processing operations in computing devices, including principles like Landauer's principle that establishes minimum energy dissipation for irreversible computational operations like copying and erasing information.
papers.target_domain_justification: Biochemical systems refers to the molecular networks and chemical reactions in living cells, specifically the receptor-readout networks where molecules bind and unbind to record cellular states through chemical reactions driven by ATP hydrolysis and other fuel molecules.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works because both computational copying (in computers) and biochemical readout (in cells) involve creating correlated states between a source and target. The paper transfers the fundamental thermodynamic principle that generating correlations requires minimum dissipation (from Landauer's computational framework) to analyze biochemical networks, showing that the same information-theoretic bounds apply to molecular copying processes in cells.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Computational thermodynamics studies abstract information processing in electronic/computational devices, while biochemical systems study molecular reactions in living cells. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study (bits vs. molecules), methodologies (computer science/physics vs. molecular biology/chemistry), and research communities, despite sharing thermodynamic principles.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the mathematical framework and core principles from computational thermodynamics (Landauer's bound, correlation-dissipation relationships) to biochemical networks. The entire analysis is built on applying computational thermodynamic bounds to derive fundamental limits on biochemical copying efficiency, making this transfer the foundation of the paper's contribution.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that biochemical readout networks are fundamentally performing the same information-theoretic operation as computational copying requires understanding the underlying mechanism of correlation generation and its thermodynamic costs, not just surface-level keyword matching between 'computation' and 'biology'.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a 2015 research paper presenting novel theoretical results at the intersection of computational thermodynamics and biochemistry, not a classic textbook example.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While applying Landauer's principle from computation to biochemical systems requires technical sophistication and cross-disciplinary insight, the fundamental connection between information copying and thermodynamic costs is well-established in both domains. The analogy is non-trivial but follows a logical extension of existing information-theoretic frameworks rather than requiring a deeply counter-intuitive conceptual leap.

papers.title: A thermodynamic framework for unified continuum models for the healing of damaged soft biological tissue
papers.abstract: When they are damaged or injured, soft biological tissues are able to self-repair and heal. Mechanics is critical during the healing process, as the damaged extracellular matrix (ECM) tends to be replaced with a new undamaged ECM supporting homeostatic stresses. Computational modeling has been commonly used to simulate the healing process. However, there is a pressing need to have a unified thermodynamics theory for healing. From the viewpoint of continuum damage mechanics, some key parameters related to healing processes, for instance, the volume fraction of newly grown soft tissue and the growth deformation, can be regarded as internal variables and have related evolution equations. This paper is aiming to establish this unified framework inspired by thermodynamics for continuum damage models for the healing of soft biological tissues. The significant advantage of the proposed model is that no \textit{ad hoc} equations are required for describing the healing process. Therefore, this new model is more concise and offers a universal approach to simulate the healing process. Three numerical examples are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model, which is in good agreement with the existing works, including an application for balloon angioplasty in an arteriosclerotic artery with a fiber cap.
papers.analogy_description: Builds a biomechanics damage model for soft tissues by mapping tissue microstructural degradation to internal variables in nonequilibrium thermodynamics, enforcing the second law and using thermodynamic potentials to define novel stress–damage evolution equations.
papers.problem: Existing computational models for healing of damaged soft biological tissue lack a unified thermodynamic framework and rely on ad hoc equations to describe healing processes, making them less concise and universal.
papers.method_name: Thermodynamic framework for tissue healing
papers.concrete_example: The paper treats key healing parameters like the volume fraction of newly grown soft tissue and growth deformation as internal variables with evolution equations derived from thermodynamic principles, similar to how continuum damage mechanics treats damage variables. The healing process is modeled using the same thermodynamic framework that governs damage evolution, where the free energy function drives both damage and healing through conjugate forces.
papers.base_domain_justification: Thermodynamics is the branch of physics that studies energy transformations, heat, work, and the fundamental laws governing equilibrium and non-equilibrium processes in physical systems. It provides mathematical frameworks for describing irreversible processes through concepts like free energy, entropy production, and internal variables.
papers.target_domain_justification: Tissue healing and regeneration is a biological process involving the repair and replacement of damaged extracellular matrix in soft biological tissues. This field studies cellular and mechanical processes during wound healing, tissue remodeling, and the restoration of homeostatic stresses in living organisms.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works by treating biological healing processes as thermodynamic processes with internal variables that evolve according to energy minimization principles. The structural similarity lies in both domains having dissipative processes that can be described through free energy potentials and conjugate forces - just as thermodynamic systems evolve toward energy minima, healing tissue evolves toward mechanical homeostasis through similar mathematical frameworks of internal variable evolution.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Thermodynamics studies energy transformations in physical systems while tissue healing studies biological repair processes in living organisms. These domains have fundamentally different objects of study (physical energy systems vs. biological regeneration) and distinct research traditions, though they can share mathematical formalisms.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the mathematical structure of thermodynamic frameworks, including free energy potentials, internal variables, dissipation inequalities, and evolution equations derived from energy principles. These thermodynamic mechanisms form the foundation of the entire healing model, replacing ad hoc equations with thermodynamically-consistent evolution laws for healing parameters.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that biological tissue healing can be governed by the same thermodynamic principles as physical damage processes requires understanding the underlying mechanism of energy-driven evolution and dissipation, not just surface-level keyword matching between 'damage' and 'healing'.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a recent 2021 paper presenting a novel unified thermodynamic framework for tissue healing, not a classic or widely-cited cross-domain application.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While applying thermodynamic frameworks to biological systems is an established approach in biophysics, extending it specifically to tissue healing with internal variables and homeostatic evolution requires non-trivial mathematical sophistication and domain insight. The connection makes sense once explained but isn't a standard textbook application, requiring creative adaptation of thermodynamic principles to capture the specific mechanics of biological repair processes.

papers.title: Theory of mechano-chemical patterning in biphasic biological tissues
papers.abstract: The formation of self-organized patterns is key to the morphogenesis of multicellular organisms, although a comprehensive theory of biological pattern formation is still lacking. Here, we propose a minimal model combining tissue mechanics to morphogen turnover and transport in order to explore new routes to patterning. Our active description couples morphogen reaction-diffusion, which impact on cell differentiation and tissue mechanics, to a two-phase poroelastic rheology, where one tissue phase consists of a poroelastic cell network and the other of a permeating extracellular fluid, which provides a feedback by actively transporting morphogens. While this model encompasses previous theories approximating tissues to inert monophasic media, such as Turing's reaction-diffusion model, it overcomes some of their key limitations permitting pattern formation via any two-species biochemical kinetics thanks to mechanically induced cross-diffusion flows. Moreover, we describe a qualitatively different advection-driven Keller-Segel instability which allows for the formation of patterns with a single morphogen, and whose fundamental mode pattern robustly scales with tissue size. We discuss the potential relevance of these findings for tissue morphogenesis.
papers.analogy_description: Constructs a new poroelastic biomechanics model by importing thermodynamic mixture theory and energy balance from thermo-poroelastic materials, treating fluid flow and solid deformation in tissues as coupled thermodynamic processes.
papers.problem: Existing theories of biological pattern formation (like Turing's reaction-diffusion) have key limitations and cannot explain certain morphogenetic patterns in multicellular tissues. A comprehensive theory that accounts for tissue mechanics, fluid transport, and morphogen dynamics is needed to understand how self-organized patterns form during development.
papers.method_name: Poroelastic biphasic tissue model
papers.concrete_example: The paper models biological tissue as a two-phase poroelastic medium where one phase is a poroelastic cell network (analogous to a porous solid matrix) and the other is permeating extracellular fluid (analogous to fluid in porous media). This framework, borrowed from soil mechanics and geophysics, allows morphogens to be actively transported by fluid flow through the tissue, creating mechanically-induced cross-diffusion that enables pattern formation with any two-species biochemical kinetics, overcoming limitations of classical Turing patterns.
papers.base_domain_justification: Poroelasticity is a field within continuum mechanics and geophysics that studies the behavior of fluid-saturated porous materials (like soil, rocks, or sponges), where a solid matrix is permeated by fluid that can flow through interconnected pores. This theory was originally developed for applications in soil mechanics, petroleum engineering, and geophysics.
papers.target_domain_justification: Developmental biology studies the morphogenesis and pattern formation in multicellular organisms during embryonic development, focusing on how cells differentiate, organize, and create structured tissues and organs through biochemical signaling and mechanical interactions.
papers.analogy_justification: The analogy works because biological tissues can be conceptualized as porous media where cells form a deformable network (solid phase) and extracellular fluid flows through intercellular spaces (fluid phase). The mathematical framework of poroelasticity, which describes stress-strain relationships and fluid transport in porous materials, directly transfers to model how mechanical forces and fluid flow in tissues affect morphogen transport and pattern formation, providing mechanisms (like advection-driven instabilities) not captured by purely chemical models.
papers.domain_distance_justification: Poroelasticity originates from geophysics and engineering (studying inanimate materials like soil and rocks), while developmental biology studies living organisms and morphogenesis. These fields have fundamentally different objects of study, distinct research communities, and historically separate methodological traditions.
papers.analogy_usage_justification: The paper directly transfers the mathematical structure of poroelastic theory, including the two-phase rheology, stress-strain relationships, and fluid transport equations, to model tissue mechanics. This poroelastic framework forms the foundation of their model and enables the key novel predictions (mechanically-induced cross-diffusion, advection-driven Keller-Segel instability). The entire theoretical apparatus is built on this transferred mathematical structure.
papers.structural_reasoning_justification: Recognizing that biological tissues can be modeled using poroelastic theory requires understanding the mechanistic parallels between fluid flow through porous geological materials and extracellular fluid transport through cellular networks. This is not semantically obvious from keywords alone but requires insight into the underlying physical mechanisms.
papers.well_known_justification: This is a recent (2018) theoretical development applying poroelastic theory to pattern formation, not a classic textbook example of cross-domain transfer.
papers.difficulty_reasoning: While poroelasticity is an established framework in engineering and geophysics, applying it to developmental biology requires non-trivial insight to recognize tissues as biphasic materials and connect mechanical-fluid dynamics to morphogen patterning. However, once the tissue-as-porous-medium conceptualization is made, the mathematical transfer is relatively systematic rather than requiring counter-intuitive leaps.

papers.title: A topological mechanism for robust and efficient global oscillations in biological networks
papers.abstract: Long and stable timescales are often observed in complex biochemical networks, such as in emergent oscillations.
